skill.name: dispatching-parallel-agents
skill.description: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users to split independent debugging or implementation work across parallel agents.
skill.summary: Plan and coordinate independent agent tasks for faster debugging and integration.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell backticks, weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance. Manual review found only Markdown examples, Graphviz syntax, and workflow guidance in SKILL.md, with no executable code, network calls, cryptography, or prompt injection attempts. The skill is safe to publish.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown code fences and examples. They show a Graphviz diagram, TypeScript-style Task examples, a prompt template, and plain text agent assignments; no shell command is executed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside Markdown fenced blocks and contains documentation examples only. No executable script, shell invocation, or user input interpolation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance and Cryptography Patterns
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, network reconnaissance, and system reconnaissance detections do not match the reviewed content. Line 3 is a skill description, line 60 says agents need a clear goal, and line 169 advises checking for edit conflicts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain ordinary prose about task coordination. No cryptographic algorithm, network scanning instruction, or system reconnaissance instruction appears there.

content.value_statement: Complex debugging slows down when independent failures are handled one at a time. This skill helps users split work into focused agent tasks, review results, and integrate fixes safely.
actual_capabilities: Helps decide whether multiple failures are independent enough for parallel work.
Provides a repeatable pattern for assigning one agent per problem domain.
Shows how to write focused agent prompts with scope, constraints, and expected output.
Explains how to review agent summaries and check for conflicting changes.
Defines cases where parallel agents should not be used.
limitations: It does not run agents by itself; the user must use an AI environment that supports agent tasks.
It does not guarantee that parallel fixes will merge without conflicts.
It works best when failures have clear boundaries and separate root causes.
It does not replace final human review and full test verification.

use_cases.title: Split Independent Test Failures
use_cases.description: Assign separate agents to unrelated failing test files and compare their root cause summaries before integration.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Multi-Subsystem Debugging
use_cases.description: Give each agent a subsystem boundary, a clear goal, and constraints that prevent unrelated refactors.

use_cases.title: Review Parallel Agent Output
use_cases.description: Use the verification checklist to detect overlapping edits, incomplete fixes, and missing full-suite validation.

prompt_templates.title: Check If Parallel Agents Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: I have these failures: [paste list]. Decide whether they are independent enough for parallel agents. Group them by problem domain and explain any dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Create Agent Assignments
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare focused tasks for separate agents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create one agent task for each independent domain. Include scope, goal, constraints, relevant files, expected output, and what each agent should avoid changing.

prompt_templates.title: Review Agent Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these agent summaries and changed files: [paste summaries]. Identify conflicts, missing verification, and the safest integration order.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Full Parallel Debugging Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a large failure set.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a parallel debugging plan for this failure set: [paste details]. Define domains, agent prompts, shared constraints, integration checks, and final verification commands.

output_examples.input: Three unrelated test files fail after a refactor.
output: Group each failing file as a separate investigation domain.
Assign one agent to each file with clear constraints and expected summary output.
Review returned changes for overlap, then run the full test suite.

output_examples.input: Two failures may share the same root cause.
output_examples.output: Investigate them together first. Parallel dispatch is not recommended until the shared dependency is understood.

output_examples.input: Agents finished independent fixes with no overlapping files.
output_examples.output: Read each summary, confirm the root cause, run focused tests, run the full suite, and then integrate the changes.

best_practices: Dispatch agents only after grouping failures by independent problem domains.
Give each agent a narrow scope, explicit constraints, and a required summary format.
Run focused checks and a full verification pass after integrating agent results.
anti_patterns: Do not ask one agent to fix every failure when domains are unrelated and bounded.
Do not dispatch parallel agents when they need to edit the same files or shared state.
Do not accept agent changes without reading summaries and checking for conflicts.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps users split independent debugging or implementation work across multiple focused agents.

faq.question: Does it execute commands or modify files?
faq.answer: No. It is a workflow guide and prompt pattern, not an executable script.

faq.question: When should I avoid parallel agents?
faq.answer: Avoid them when failures share a root cause, need shared context, or require editing the same resources.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is written for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that support agent-style task delegation.

faq.question: How should agent prompts be structured?
faq.answer: Use a focused scope, clear goal, constraints, relevant context, and a specific expected output.

faq.question: What should happen after agents return?
faq.answer: Review summaries, check for conflicting edits, run focused tests, and run the full test suite.
